title: Survey
text:
We’re currently conducting a survey to gather feedback on the proposed Tentsmuir Cycle Path. Your input is crucial in designing a route that caters to both the community, commuters and visitors.
We’ll submit the survey results to Fife Council as part of our campaign to secure their support and agreement to build the Tentsmuir Cycle Link.
The survey only takes five minutes and includes an opportunity to add any additional thoughts or ideas at the end. You can access via the link below 👇;

 Complete the Survey Now

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text: Got questions about the Tentsmuir Cycle Link? We’ve answered the most common ones below. If you’d like to ask something else, feel free to contact us.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
About the Route

What is the Tentsmuir Cycle Link?
The Tentsmuir Cycle Link is a proposed cycle and walking path that would connect Tayport and Leuchars, completing a continuous, safe route for cyclists and pedestrians all the way from Dundee to St Andrews.
Cycle paths already exist between Dundee and Tayport, and between Leuchars and St Andrews. This new link fills the missing middle section — approximately 9 km — running alongside Tentsmuir Forest.


Why does the path run between Tayport and Leuchars specifically?
This section is the critical missing link in an otherwise continuous route from Dundee to St Andrews. Currently there is no safe off-road option for this stretch. Cyclists are forced onto the A914, which is a busy, fast, and narrow road that many people — particularly families, children, and older adults — find far too dangerous.
Completing this connection would unlock the entire Dundee–St Andrews corridor for safe active travel.


How long is the proposed route?
The route between Tayport and Leuchars is approximately 9 km (around 5.5 miles). At a relaxed cycling pace this takes roughly 25–45 minutes.


Where exactly would the path go?
The path would run alongside Tentsmuir Forest, one of Scotland’s most beautiful natural landscapes. You can explore the proposed alignment in detail on our Map page.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
Why Build It?

Why can’t people just use the existing roads?
The current road options between Tayport and Leuchars are unsuitable for most cyclists:

The A914 is a busy, fast-moving road with no dedicated cycling provision and narrow verges.
Many people — including children, families, and older or less confident cyclists — simply won’t use it.
The road also suffers from potholes and poor sightlines in places.

A safe, dedicated off-road path is needed to make cycling a genuine everyday option for local residents.


Haven’t bus services recently been cut in the area?
Yes. Recent cuts to bus services in Tayport and Leuchars have left many residents with fewer options for getting around without a car. While the cycle path won’t directly replace bus services, it provides an important and reliable alternative for shorter local journeys — particularly to Leuchars railway station — that people can use regardless of timetables or fares.


How would the path help people get to Leuchars railway station?
Leuchars railway station sits on the main Edinburgh–Dundee–Aberdeen rail line and provides fast connections to Edinburgh and beyond. Currently, accessing the station from Tayport without a car is difficult.
The cycle path would make it straightforward to cycle or walk to the station in 25–45 minutes, opening up rail travel for commuters and day-trippers who would otherwise need to drive and park.


Would this benefit people who don’t currently cycle?
Absolutely — and that’s one of the main goals. Research consistently shows that people don’t cycle primarily because roads feel unsafe, not because they lack interest. A safe, off-road path removes the biggest barrier. Many people who wouldn’t dream of cycling on the A914 would be happy to use a well-surfaced, separated path through Tentsmuir.
The path would also be fully accessible on foot, so it benefits walkers, families with buggies, and people with mobility aids too.


Will it bring more visitors to Tayport?
Yes. The path would make Tayport much more accessible by bike from Leuchars, St Andrews, and Dundee. This increased footfall would benefit local shops, cafes, pubs and businesses, providing a boost to the local economy.
It would also give visitors a new and attractive way to access Tentsmuir Forest and Beach — one of Fife’s most popular natural destinations.


Is the route only useful for leisure cycling?
No — the route is designed for everyday transport as well as leisure. It would be a practical option for:

Commuters cycling to Leuchars station or to workplaces in Leuchars (including RAF Leuchars / MOD Leuchars)
Students and families travelling between communities
Shoppers and visitors travelling between Tayport, Leuchars and St Andrews
Recreational cyclists and walkers exploring Tentsmuir

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
Environment & Health

How does the path help reduce CO₂ emissions?
Every car journey replaced by a bike ride reduces carbon emissions. Studies have shown that investment in cycling infrastructure can produce significant and sustained reductions in car use. By making cycling a safe and practical choice for everyday trips, the Tentsmuir Cycle Link would contribute meaningfully to reducing the area’s carbon footprint and help meet Scotland’s climate targets.


What are the health benefits?
Regular cycling and walking improve both physical and mental health. A safe local cycle path encourages more people — including those who currently do very little exercise — to build active travel into their daily routines. This has knock-on benefits for the NHS and for individuals’ long-term wellbeing.


Will the path be good for wildlife?
Yes. Alongside the path, we plan to plant native hedgerows and wildflowers, creating a living wildlife corridor between Tayport and Leuchars. This will support insects, birds and other wildlife — turning the route into a biodiversity asset as well as a transport one.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
The Project

Who is behind this campaign?
The campaign is run by a group of local residents and cycling enthusiasts who want to see Tayport, Leuchars and the surrounding communities better connected by safe active travel routes. We work under the name Tentsmuir Cycle and engage with Fife Council and other stakeholders to push the project forward.


Where has the project got to so far?
There has been encouraging progress behind the scenes, including early engagement with Fife Council and local representatives. You can read more about the story so far on our blog.


How can I help?
The single most impactful thing you can do right now is take our survey. Demonstrating local support with real numbers is vital for making the case to Fife Council that this route should be prioritised.

 Take our Survey! 

You can also spread the word by sharing the website with friends, family and neighbours, especially those who live in Tayport, Leuchars and St Andrews.


Who can I contact if I have more questions?
Feel free to get in touch by email — we’d love to hear from you.

title: About
text: This site provides information about the Tentsmuir Cycle Path project, including news, updates, and resources.

title: Route Map
text:
We are working on several different possible routes. Three of the broad options are detailed in the interactive map below. Our preferred option is to follow the old railway line (dashed blue). Any of the routes are subject to change with feasibility, practicality, cost and land owner consent all factors in what is finally built.
Click on any route to see more details. The dashed lines are possible routes and solid lines are existing cycle paths.

title: Route Map
text:
Route details

Option A – Old Railway Line (Preferred)
Distance: 6.7 km | Status: Preferred route
Option A follows the route of the former Tayport Branch Line, which closed in 1966. The trackbed is largely flat and intact, offering a direct, traffic-free corridor between Tayport and Leuchars. Key advantages include:

Minimal road crossings, improving safety for all users
Low impact on agricultural land compared to the other options
Likely the least expensive route to construct due to the existing formation
The historic railway alignment is well understood and largely in public or sympathetic ownership

This is our preferred option and the one we are actively promoting with Fife Council and Transport Scotland.



Option B – Tentsmuir Forest Route: 8.0 km
Distance: 8.0 km | Status: Alternative under consideration
Option B runs through the interior of Tentsmuir Forest before joining the back road to Leuchars. While it avoids private land, it comes with several challenges:

The back road to Leuchars is narrow and would need to be widened at several points
The route is 1.3 km longer than Option A
Road widening works would be more disruptive and costly than the railway option
Sections through the forest may be affected by forestry operations

This option remains under consideration but is not currently preferred.



Option C – Tentsmuir Route: 8.9 km
Distance: 8.9 km | Status: Alternative under consideration
Option C is a longer alternative through Tentsmuir suggested by some stakeholders. It shares many of the same challenges as Option B, and is an additional 2.2 km compared to the preferred railway route. A large portion of this route would follow an existing forestry track, which offers some advantage, but:

The overall length makes it less attractive for everyday cycling trips
It still requires road sections that would need improvement
The additional distance reduces its competitiveness as a practical commuter or leisure route

This option is retained for completeness but is not preferred at this stage.



Active travel strategy
The Tayport Cycle Link forms part of Fife Council’s broader Active Travel Strategy connecting Fife’s towns and villages with safe cycle routes. You can find more information about the Active Travel Strategy on the Fife Council website.


Map of Fife Council’s Active Travel Strategy showing proposed cycle routes in North East Fife

title: Website Launched
text: Welcome to our new website! Please have a look around and check out our first blog post for some background on the project.

title: Benefits
text:
We believe the Tentsmuir Cycle Link is a vital piece of community infrastructure that will bring numerous benefits to North East Fife and beyond.

🌱 Connecting our Communities
The Tentsmuir Cycle Link will mean there is a continuous, safe and efficient cycle path from St Andrews to Dundee and beyond. This link between towns such as Tayport and Leuchars, will allow more access to local services without the need to rely on buses, bring more business to Tayport and Leuchars, make safe cycle commuting possible and generally enhance community connectivity in North East Fife.

Providing our communities with transport options
The Tentsmuir Cycle Link will provide an alternative to car travel for many local journeys. This is especially important given the recent cuts to bus services in Tayport and Leuchars - although it won’t replace these services it will provide options for the local community.


Access to Leuchars Railway Station
It will be a roughly 9km (25 - 45 mins) cycle along the cycle path from Tayport to Leuchars railway station. This will make it easier to commute by train to Edinburgh and beyond without the need for a car.


More footfall in Tayport
The cycle path will bring more people into Tayport, especially from Leuchars and St Andrews. This will result in more business for local shops, cafes and pubs.


Options to access Tentsmuir Forest and Beach
The Tentsmuir Cycle Link will provide a safe and efficient way for people to access Tentsmuir Forest and Beach by bike or on foot from Leuchars, Tayport and St Andrews. This will open up this beautiful area to more people without the need for a car.



🛡️ Safer for Everyone
Lots of people are put off cycling by our pot holed and sometimes dangerous roads. Currently the options to cycle between St Andrews and Dundee via Tayport involves either a very busy and narrow road or taking a long detour into Tentsmuir forest on gravel track unsuitable for road bikes. The Tentsmuir Cycle link will provide a continuous, segregated, efficient link for cyclists avoiding busy roads meaning a safer journey for cyclists and less congestion and frustration for cars.


🏃 Boosts Health and Active Lifestyles
Cycle paths encourage more cycling and active travel boosting health. The Tentsmuir Cycle link will encourage community and open up cycling destinations via bike to far more people. It will be a beautiful route passing through forests and countryside and studies show that even just ten minutes in nature can greatly boost mental health and well being.


🌳 A Biodiversity Corridor
We plan on planting native hedges and wildflowers alongside the path encouraging insects, birds and other wildlife and creating a wildlife corridor between Leuchars and Tayport.


🌱 Cuts CO₂ Emissions
Cycle paths take cars off the road and studies have shown they can significantly reduce carbon emissions helping us reach a less carbon-intensive society.

title: The Story so Far
text:
🚴️ Tracing the Route of the Tentsmuir Cycle Link
This project began with a simple (and not new) idea: exploring the potential for a new, dedicated route for active travel in our community, connecting Tayport to Leuchars.
I had been working in Denmark for a few years where cycling infrastructure is second to none and had seen first hand the huge benefits to communities in terms of health, well being, reduced congestion and choice that good segregated cycle paths bring. But, living in Tayport,there was a real lack of options for safe cycling locally towards St Andrews, which is an economic hub in this part of Scotland. The main road between Tayport and Leuchars is narrow, busy and has no cycle lanes - not suitable for families or less confident cyclists. The alternative route via Tentsmuir is lovely, but runs along on gravel tracks unsuitable for road bikes and is a significant detour. It seemed crazy not to have a direct safe option, especially when a good route existed between Dundee and Tayport and St Andrews to Leuchars - if we could get a link between Tayport and Leuchars we could connect all these places with a safe, efficient route for cycling and walking.


🗺️ The First Exploration: South from the Larick
I heard through the Tayport community gardens that Mark was well connected to cycle communities - I approached Mark at a meeting in Larick center, asking if anyone had thought about a potential path along the old railway line from Tayport to Leuchars. Turns out indeed many had but there had never been much progress. So Mark, Jim (who Mark introduced me too) and I decided to go for a cold February walk from the Larick Centre, heading south down the Scott & Fyfe road to see if we could follow the old railway line.
While a significant portion of the route was plain sailing, the initial stages required a bit of inventive navigation! We squeezed under a few barbed wire fences, carefully crossed a short stretch of a planted winter barley field (responsibly of course), jumped one ditch, and diverted around a field boundary.Eventually we made to a section of the railway which is relatively well preserved (Morton Lochs also has a great section that’s currently a path) near the Rhynd cafe and from there it was an easy walk to Leuchars.



Part of the old railway in Morton Lochs




🚧 Alternative Route Challenges
In other walks we also explored some alternative routes but these were not always straightforward. Mark and Jim attempted to map an alternative route from the south, which proved to be a chilly lesson in the lay of the land.
They were cut off by a high deer fence and gate near Fetterdale. Jim managed to climb the gate, but Mark found it tricky. After they climbed back over, they had to change tack, returning to the Rhynd area. There, they encountered another obstacle—a lower locked gate—which was climbed to finally gain access to the Tentsmuir forest track and get back up to Fetterdale. Despite the cold, they returned pleased, having learned invaluable lessons about the landscape and potential access points.


🏛️ Laying the Groundwork: Consultation and Research
After exploring the possible options on foot Jim and I got on our laptops and started trying to figure out how to make the cycle path a reality. By happy coincidence, Fife Council had just begun a consultation on the Active Travel Strategy and Action Plan. We attended a consultation meeting in the small meeting room at the Larick Centre, which helped us align our vision with the broader local strategy - we submitted our thoughts as a document to the consultation that a direct link between Leuchars and Tayport would be important and the railway line was an obvious place to start thinking about where a route might go. We then began detailed research:

Landowner Identification: We researched online to find the landowners involved along the route.
Historical Mapping: We poured over maps of the old railway line to trace the forgotten path, a potential alignment for our route.
Formal Submissions: Our initial research culminated in a succesful submission to Fife Council’s Active Travel Strategy.

We also reached out to Cllr Jonny Tepp, asking if he had any advice. His insight have been greatly appreciated as we navigate the complexities of local planning.
Note: Cllr Tepp maintains a reasonable ‘arms length’ from the official process to retain his neutrality as Convener of the North East Fife Area Committee and avoid being barred from voting on any future planning application.


🤝 Building Relationships: Engaging with the Farming Community
In establishing this route it is important that we engage with landowners. While it’s always going to be tricky trying to approach the idea of a path being built on someone’s land, all the landowners we have approached so far have listened to our thoughts and ideas and we hope this forged an initial relationship for future discussions. Our aim is to work collaboratively and keep them in the loop at every stage.


🌟 Bigger and Team and moving Forward
Our numbers are growing with two new team members, Jenny and Shona, and we are now a constituted body within Tayport Community Council that can represent the community in Tayport along with Guardbridge and Leuchars.
Our next stage is…well this blog post the new website and community consultation. Once we show community buy-in, Fife council should release the funding for an initial feasibility study and that will push us closer to safer, more accessible active travel for Tayport, Leuchars and North East Fife.

title: Welcome to the Tentsmuir Cycle Link Campaign
text:
Why a Cyclepath?

  
    🌱 Cuts CO₂ Emissions
    Every bike ride means fewer car journeys, helping keep our air clean and our planet healthy.
  
  
    🏃️ Boosts Health
    Cycling and walking are great ways to stay active and improve mental wellbeing.
  
  
    🛡️ Safer for All
    Separated paths offer a safer space for cyclists, pedestrians, and families.
  
  
    🚗 Reduces Road Traffic
    Fewer cyclists on busy roads means less congestion and smoother journeys for everyone.
  
  
    🌳 Encourages Active Lifestyles
    Enjoy nature, meet neighbours, and discover the joy of traveling at your own pace.
